subject: Cloud Computing
title: The Uber 2016 Cover-Up
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Uber 2016 Cover-Up usable.

hook.text: Hackers stole 57 million Uber user records in 2016. Uber's response: pay the hackers $100,000 and hide the breach.

buildup.text: The attackers found AWS credentials in a GitHub repository. With those keys, they accessed Uber's S3 data.

discovery.text: Uber paid the hackers through its bug bounty program, disguising the ransom as a legitimate security payout.

twist.text: The cover-up lasted a year. When it was exposed, the CSO was fired and later criminally charged for obstruction.

climax.text: The lesson: breaches happen. Cover-ups destroy companies. Transparency and rapid response are the only acceptable path.

punchline.text: The breach was bad. The cover-up was worse.

quiz.question: How did attackers initially access Uber's cloud data?
options: They found AWS credentials in a public GitHub repository
They guessed the CEO's password after 10 attempts
Uber accidentally made all S3 buckets publicly accessible
